title: Systems and methods for intraoperatively measuring anatomical orientation
abstract: Systems and methods are disclosed in which changes in the position and/or orientation of an anatomical structure or of a surgical tool can be measured quantitatively during surgery. In some embodiments, the systems and methods disclosed herein can make use of inertial motion sensors to determine a position or orientation of an instrument or anatomy at different times and to calculate changes between different positions or orientations. In other embodiments, such sensors can be utilized in conjunction with imaging devices to correlate sensor position with anatomical landmarks, thereby permitting determination of absolute angular orientation of a landmark. Such systems and methods can facilitate real-time tracking of progress during a variety of procedures, including, e.g., spinal deformity correction, etc.
claims:
Claims What is claimed is: 1. A surgical electronic module, comprising: a housing; a sensor disposed in the housing and configured to detect a position or orientation of the module; an input disposed on an outer surface of the housing; a processor coupled to the sensor and configured to record the position or orientation of the module in response to instruction from the input, wherein the processor is further configured to simultaneously calculate a change in position or orientation of the module in two orthogonal planes with respect to a reference point based on the position or orientation detected by the sensor; and a display configured to display the change calculated by the processor to thereby assist a user in assessing changes in position or orientation of anatomy coupled to the module. 2. The module of claim 1, wherein the housing includes an engagement feature configured to removably attach the housing to a surgical instrument. 3. The module of claim 1, wherein the module is integrally formed in a surgical instrument. 4. The module of claim 2, wherein the instrument is any of an osteotome, a chisel, a deformity correction instrument, a probe, an awl, a drill, a tap, and a gearshift. 5. The module of claim 1, wherein the sensor is an mertial motion sensor including any of an accelerometer, a gyroscope, and a magnetometer. 6. The module of claim 1, wherein the input is a single button. 7. The module of claim 1 , wherein the input includes a plurality of buttons. 8. The module of claim 7, wherein each of the plurality of buttons can be selectively lighted to prompt a user for input. 9. The module of claim 1, wherein the module includes a reference plane alignment feature for defining the two orthogonal planes. 10. A surgical method, comprising: positioning a surgical instrument in a first position or orientation relative to anatomy; receiving instruction from an input and recording the first position or orientation using a sensor coupled to the surgical instrument and configured to detect a position or orientation thereof: positioning the surgical instrument in a second position or orientation relative to anatomy; receiving instruction from the input and recording the second position or orientation using the sensor; calculating changes in position or orientation between the first and second positions or orientations in two orthogonal planes; displaying the changes in position or orientation in the two orthogonal planes using a display coupled to the surgical instrument. 11. The method of claim 10, further comprising coupling a surgical electronic module including the sensor and the display to the surgical instrument. 12. The method of claim 10, wherein the surgical instrument is any of an osteotome, a chisel, a deformity correction instrument, a probe, an awl, a drill, a tap, and a gearshift. 13. The method of claim 10, wherein the input is a single button. 14. The method of claim 10, wherein the input includes a plurality of buttons. 15. The method of claim 14, further comprising resetting any of the recorded first and second positions or orientations in response to simultaneous depression of more than one button. 16. The method of claim 14, selectively illuminating different ones of the plurality of buttons to guide a user through recording the first and second positions or orientations. 17. The method of claim 10, further comprising: positioning the surgical instrument in a third position or orientation relative to anatomy; receiving instruction from the input and recording the third position or orientation using the sensor; 

 positioning the surgical instrument in a fourth position or orientation relative to anatomy; receiving instruction from the input and recording the fourth position or orientation using the sensor; calculating changes in position or orientation between the third and fourth positions or orientations as projected onto a first plane defined between the first and second positions or orientations and a second plane orthogonal to the first plane; displaying the changes in position or orientation between the third and fourth positions or orientations using the display. 18. A method for performing bone or tissue correction or manipulation, comprising: coupling first and second sensors to respective first and second portions of a patient's anatomy; actuating an imaging device to capture an image of the first and second portions of the anatomy with the first and second sensors coupled thereto; utilizing the image of the first and second portions of the anatomy in a simulated environment to identify a desired anatomical landmark with respect to each of the attached sensors; using a processor, determining a compensatory angle between each sensor and its respective anatomical landmark; and utilizing the compensatory angles and a measured angle between the first and second sensors to calculate an absolute anatomical angular orientation of the landmarks. 19. The method of claim 18, wherein coupling the first and second sensors comprises implanting the first and second sensors in the patient. 20. The method of claim 19, wherein the first and second sensors are percutaneously implanted in the patient, 21. The method of claim 18, wherein the absolute angular orientation is calculated in realtime. 22. The method of claim 18, further comprising coupling a reference plane sensor to a patient's anatomy to define a reference plane in which angular orientation can be measured. 23. A method of measuring an absolute spinal angle, comprising: coupling first and second sensors to respective first and second portions of a patient's spme; capturing at least one image of the first and second sensors and the patient's spme using an imaging device; identifying in the at least one captured image first and second vertebral endplates that define the absolute spinal angle to be measured; calculating, by a processor, a first compensation angle between the first endpiate and the first sensor in the at least one captured image; calculating, by a processor, a second compensation angle between the second endpiate and the second sensor in the at least one captured image; obtaining a relative angle measured between the first and second sensors; adjusting, by a processor, the relative angle based on the first and second compensation angles to calculate the absolute spinal angle; and communicating the absolute spinal angle to a user. 24. The method of claim 23, wherein the absolute spinal angle is calculated and communicated to the user in real-time or substantially in real-time. 25. The method of claim 23, wherein the absolute spinal angle is at least one of lumbar lordosis and thoracic kyphosis. 26. The method of claim 23, wherein communicating the absolute spinal angle comprises displaying the angle on an electronic display. 27. The method of claim 23, wherein identifying the endplates comprises interacting with a graphical user interface that displays the at least one captured image to select the endplates with an input device. 28. The method of claim 23, wherein coupling the first and second sensors comprises implanting the first and second sensors in the patient. 29. The method of claim 28, wherein the first and second sensors are percutaneously implanted in the patient. 30. A surgical system, comprising: a first sensor configured to couple to a first portion of a patient's anatomy; a second sensor configured to couple a second portion of the patient's anatomy: an imaging device configured to capture an image of the patient's anatomy and the first and second sensors; a processor configured to receive via an input identification of a desired anatomical landmark with respect to one of the first and second sensors, and determine a compensatory angle between each sensor and its respective anatomical landmark, wherein the processor is further configured to calculate an absolute anatomical angular orientation of the landmarks utilizing the compensatory angles and a measured angle between the first and second sensors; a display configured to display the captured image and calculated absolute anatomical angular orientation of the landmarks. 31. The system of claim 30, further comprising a reference plane sensor configured to be coupled to the patient's anatomy to define a reference plane in which angular orientation can be measured. 32. The system of claim 31, wherein the reference plane sensor includes an orientation indicator to aid a user in aligning the reference plane sensor. 33. The system of claim 30, wherein each of the first and second sensors is an inertial motion sensor including any of an acceierometer, a gyroscope, and a magnetometer. 34. The system of claim 30, wherein each of the first and second sensors are radiopaque. The system of claim 30, further comprising first and second mounts configured to permit percutaneous coupling of the first and second sensors to the patient's anatomy; wherein each of the first and second mounts includes a needle-shaped distal portion for percutaneous insertion through a patient's skm and a proximal recess configured to receive one of the first and second sensors therewithin.
